response: Decision recorded above: **300 requests/day**, with a hard revisit checkpoint at 90 days post-Pro-launch keyed to cap-hit rate and conversion/churn data. I've also saved this decision and the revisit trigger to memory for future reference.

options.label: Cap at 1,000/day
options.summary: Set the free-tier cap at 1,000 requests/day, matching the already-built and load-tested metering capacity.

options.label: Cap near observed usage (~150/day)
options.summary: Set the cap close to typical active-key usage (~100-250/day) so it functions as a conversion lever toward Pro.

options.label: Intermediate cap with planned revisit
options.summary: Choose a cap between observed usage and built capacity, with a defined point to reassess once Pro conversion data exists.

O1.crux: At 1,000/day (~10x even the busiest day's ~106/key average), the cap never binds, so it can't function as the conversion lever the memo says the whole decision is about.
O1.dependency: If the free tier isn't meant to drive Pro conversion at all — e.g. the goal is just frictionless launch with zero support tickets about caps — then reusing the already-built, load-tested 1,000 ceiling is the low-risk default and this score should rise.

O2.crux: A ~150/day cap sits just above typical usage (~100/key) but below busiest days (~250), so it plausibly binds active users within weeks and creates real upgrade pressure, matching the memo's stated purpose.
O2.dependency: If hitting the wall mostly produces churn/complaints rather than upgrades (because Pro's value prop isn't yet proven to free users), this aggressive a cap could cost more users than it converts, reversing the score downward.

O3.crux: Given the memo's own admission that conversion elasticity is an unvalidated guess, picking a middle cap with a defined revisit point buys the conversion-lever effect while capping downside risk from guessing wrong in either direction.
O3.dependency: If the 'intermediate' number chosen ends up far above observed usage (e.g. 500-600/day), it fails to bind just like O1 and the revisit-later plan becomes the only thing doing real work — so the score hinges on the specific number actually being close enough to observed usage to matter now, not just on having a revisit plan.

O1.crux: 1,000/day is ~10x the ~90-106/day even the busiest observed day hits, so the cap essentially never binds and the free tier stays functionally unlimited.
O1.dependency: Reverses to a 4-5 if the founder's real goal is generosity/goodwill rather than conversion — e.g., if Pro's fair-use-unmetered pitch depends on free also feeling uncapped, matching built capacity at zero extra engineering cost becomes the right call.

O2.crux: Setting the cap at ~150/day (just above the ~100 typical and near-ish the ~106 busiest-day peak) puts it directly in the path of real usage, so it actually functions as the conversion lever the memo says the whole decision hinges on.
O2.dependency: Reverses toward a 2 if existing free users interpret a sudden tight cap as a bait-and-switch (they've had five months of effectively unlimited free use) and churn outright instead of converting — there's no conversion data yet to confirm upgrade elasticity beats churn risk.

O3.crux: The memo states plainly that conversion elasticity is 'a guess' with zero Pro data yet, so committing to either extreme now is premature — an intermediate cap with a named revisit point buys the missing data before locking in.
O3.dependency: Reverses to a 2-3 if 'revisit later' isn't credible for a solo founder with no forcing function — an undefined or perpetually-deferred revisit just becomes O1 by default, inheriting O1's weakness without ever collecting the data that justified picking O3.

O1.crux: 1,000/day is ~10x the observed per-key daily average (~94-106), so it never binds on any real free user and can't function as the conversion lever the memo says the cap's purpose is
O1.dependency: reverses if the founder's real goal here is minimizing launch friction/cost rather than engineering a conversion lever, since reusing the already-built, load-tested number is then the correct low-effort choice

O2.crux: 150/day sits just above the tightly clustered observed per-key average (min 94.2, max 106.0 across 30 days), so it would bind on nearly all active users within weeks and directly serves the memo's stated conversion-lever purpose
O2.dependency: reverses if per-key usage is heavy-tailed rather than uniform — the CSV only gives aggregate daily average per key, not the distribution of individual keys, so if a subset of legitimate users spike well above 150 on given days, this cap would choke good-faith heavy usage as often as it nudges upgrades

O3.crux: it explicitly hedges the memo's central admitted uncertainty ('how strongly the cap drives upgrades is a guess') by pairing an intermediate number with a defined reassessment trigger once real Pro conversion data exists
O3.dependency: reverses if the 'planned revisit' is never operationalized with a concrete date/trigger — without that, O3 collapses into an arbitrary number picked with the same zero conversion data as O2, just with added complexity and no real advantage
